text.title: AI-Native Product Development — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every AI-native product strategy enforces: human-refines as a first-class feature, inference-cost as first-class metric, pinned model versions, scored build-vs-buy, EU AI Act compliance hooks.

rule.statement: The product spec MUST treat the "human refines" step as a product feature with its own UX + telemetry, NOT as a fallback or workaround. User refinements are training signal and must be captured.

rationale.source: AI-native product patterns; RLHF and refinement-as-signal literature

rule.rationale: The "human refines" step is the highest-quality training signal for future model improvement. Treating it as a workaround wastes the most valuable feedback in the system.

rule.statement: Inference cost MUST appear in the product dashboard alongside DAU / retention / NPS. Roadmap features that improve engagement but degrade $/active-user by >10% require explicit unit-economics sign-off.

rationale.source: AI unit-economics post-mortems; agent COGS case studies

rule.rationale: AI-native products scale inference cost with usage. A feature that drives engagement but doubles per-user inference cost may be net-negative on unit economics.

rule.statement: Every AI component in the spec MUST pin an exact model identifier (e.g. `claude-opus-4.6@2026-04-01`). Using "latest" or "best available" is rejected.
rule.rationale: "Latest" is not a version. Unversioned models change behaviour without a code release. AI-native roadmaps must include model upgrade testing as a recurring task.

rule.statement: Every build-vs-buy decision MUST be scored on (a) core-differentiator? (b) unique-data-advantage? (c) latency / cost constraint not met by vendor? Build only when (a) AND (b) are true. Decisions carry a 12-18 month revisit date.

rationale.source: Build-vs-buy case studies; AI vendor commoditisation trends

rule.rationale: Build-vs-buy decisions have a 12-18 month shelf life and must be revisited systematically. Without scoring, teams build common capabilities they could rent.

rule.statement: Each AI component MUST be classified against EU AI Act tiers (prohibited / high-risk / limited / minimal). High-risk components require documented explainability + bias-testing + human-oversight obligations in the spec.
rule.rationale: Compliance cannot be deferred to legal review pre-launch. Risk classification must precede architecture choices; remediation post-fact is more expensive than design-time accommodation.

example.p: Roadmap line: "Design-prototype AI generator. Model: claude-opus-4.6@2026-04-01. Risk tier: limited (transparency only). Build (core differentiator + proprietary design corpus). Revisit: 2027-04-01. Inference $0.12/active-user-day."
Roadmap line: "Add AI design generator (latest model)." — no version pin, no risk classification, no build-vs-buy rationale, no inference cost.
